questions.stem: A team deploys a new version of their booking agent to production, but customers report errors. The team needs to revert quickly. What happens when they click Rollback on the problematic deployment?

options.text: The problematic deployment is deleted and all active sessions are immediately terminated
The current deployment is retired and the previous deployment is reactivated; existing sessions on the old deployment complete naturally during the draining period
The agent code is automatically reverted to the last Git commit

questions.explanation: Rollback retires the current problematic deployment and reactivates the previous one. The deployment lifecycle uses three statuses: active (receiving new sessions), draining (no new sessions but existing sessions complete naturally), and retired (fully decommissioned). This ensures no customer conversations are abruptly cut off during the transition.

questions.stem: A company wants to test agent changes in a production-like environment before real customers see them. They want to use the same tools and LLM configuration as production. Which Agent Platform workflow supports this?

options.text: Test directly in the Studio chat panel, which is identical to production
Deploy to the staging environment with production-like configuration, validate with eval sets, then promote to production
Create a separate workspace that mirrors production settings

questions.explanation: The platform supports three environments per project: dev, staging, and production. Each environment has its own deployments, environment variables, channel bindings, and model overrides. Deploy to staging with production-like configuration, run eval sets to validate, then promote to production. The promote API copies the staging deployment to production without rebuilding.

questions.stem: The ___ metric on the Agent Platform Insights dashboard measures the percentage of sessions resolved without human escalation, indicating how effectively agents handle conversations autonomously.
questions.explanation: Containment Rate is one of the five KPI metric cards on the Insights dashboard. It measures the percentage of sessions that reached a successful outcome without requiring a human agent. A high containment rate indicates the AI agent is effectively handling conversations. The other KPI cards show Sessions, Messages, Tokens, and Estimated Cost.

questions.stem: A DevOps team wants to automate deployments so that every push to the 'main' Git branch automatically deploys to production. Which Agent Platform capability enables this?

options.text: Environment variable feature flags that toggle agent behavior
Git integration with auto-deploy configured to trigger on pushes to the main branch, which auto-versions, compiles, validates, and deploys agents
A scheduled deployment that checks the repository every hour

questions.explanation: The Git integration supports auto-deploy: when a push lands on the configured branch, the platform automatically pulls the latest files, validates and compiles all agents, creates auto-versioned snapshots, and creates a new deployment. Teams can map different branches to different environments (develop to dev, staging to staging, main to production) for a full GitOps workflow.

questions.stem: A project manager notices LLM costs doubled last month. Which Agent Platform analytics feature helps identify the root cause?

options.text: The trace viewer, which shows individual session execution details
The Insights dashboard cost breakdown table, which shows spending by agent, plus the billing page breakdown by model and daily usage trends to identify cost spikes
The guardrail audit tab, which tracks content violations

questions.explanation: The Insights dashboard includes a cost breakdown table grouped by agent name, sessions handled, tokens consumed, and estimated cost. The billing page adds breakdowns by provider, by model (with input/output tokens and cost), and a daily usage trend chart to spot spikes. Together, these identify whether the cost increase comes from a specific agent, a model change, or a usage spike.
